proposal_text:
Proposal number: 1333885

Proposal: LET'S WALK! - climate change awareness day event

Summary


Purpose: helping to rise the climate change awareness our world so urgently needs. 

Suggested actions: During that day we encourage people to walk and to:

leave their cars at home,
skip the bus,
avoid the subway and
turn to their own bodies in order to move around the city, by walking, by using their bicycles, their rollers, their skates.
If you are not lucky enough to be living near your work space you can still collaborate by doing home office and other activities such as walking to the grocery or the club.
Using a smartphone app we intend to track how much people walk during the day of the event. This information will then be published by social media and traditional media. The data provided by the app will also be translate in the reductions already mentioned, calculating savings in relationship  with the kilometrers walked and avoiding using transportation during that day.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page [14].
Because of the altruistic spirit of the cause, we expect to have support from the local ONG´s, health organizations and the government.     
Main value generation loops v1.0.png


Short term Impacts: we expect to save energy and money, GHG emissions, and we will contribute to build sustainable habits, for a healthy environment in the war against the dangers of a sedentary lifestyle.  

Long term impacts:

Since the proposal is to make people aware of how important it is to care about the planet. More climate change aware people would naturally support the following initiatives:

Recycling: We forecast that it could increase by 10 %,
 
Energy savings: recommendations use increment by 10 %
 
Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law: is approved and covers 5 % of city demand.
 
Electric buses: their use is implemented in 2 % of city bus lines.
Is this proposal for a practice or a project?
Practice

What actions do you propose?
a) The main thrust of “Let’s Walk” is walking, because Buenos Aires has a high population density, and distances might be shorter than in other cities, so walking to work is a real possibility for some people. People can

Walk 10 - 15 blocks to their offices or back home.

Use public transportation, including buses, underground / subways and then walk the rest of the way to their offices or homes.

 

b) Cycling also adds value to the “Let’s walk” proposal for the population 10 to 30 blocks from their office. The Buenos Aires City already has implemented an “ecobici” (eco bikes) program [15]. The ecobici system has bikes parked in several distributed city points, and anyone can use one of these bikes for free using his / her document. You just return the bike in another bike parking place near your destination.

 

REVIEW OF SOME “BIKE TO WORK” PROGRAMS IN CANADA AND USA:

USA Today informs that Bike to Work programs might be rising in US [21].

Through a google search we found the following relevant links:

table-bike-to-work-projects.png


ANSWERS TO SOME OBJECTIONS TO “BIKE TO WORK”

The Huffpost article [16] mentions 9 objections to “Bike to work”, here are our thoughts about them!

main-objections.png

“LET’S WALK BUSINESS MODEL CANVAS”

LET`S WALK PROJECT BUSINESS CANVAS v2.0.png

As we know we have different partners in this event, which play a fundamental role.

On one side, the people enrolled in the event, that will participate registering using a mobile app. They will either walk to their works or commute by bike. In order to be eligible to receive a prize, each participant will allow the app to register information about distances walked / biked, which transportation they usually use to commute in order to calculate GHG emission savings due to the event. Those people that contribute with the biggest GHG emission savings will win prizes as well as  benefits from companies that sponsor the event.

It´s necessary to distinguish, that by incentivizing the movement we are preventing obesity, decrease bad cholesterol and increase good cholesterol, improving the state of mind and incorporating healthy habits, so typically HMOs and the city government will be very much interested, as in Argentina poor people use public hospitals maintained by city taxes.

On the other side, we need to be able to organize this event with which we need the Government's support to convey the event to the society. It will be benefited by an increased use of the public transport by the population, better flow of the traffic (less cars in the streets)  and at the same time it will reaffirm it´s commitment with the environment.

In addition to this, it will be a great opportunity to advertise the use of the eco-bikes(“ecobicis”) [15] and, as a result, the bike lanes.

On the other hand, the information generated by the app (big data)  will be of great utility since it will show which are the population's needs (for example: the best locations for entrances of subways , routes of buses or even which are the areas that are disconnected and need public transport).

It is also important to have the companies that will join on this great event allowing the use of “home office”. In addition, they will collaborate as sponsors and obtain in exchange Big Data to improve its business and find niche.

Sponsorships are one of the best ways of promoting a business and winning popularity between the target and generate good marketing for a brand. It can be specially used to increase credibility, image and prestige of the brand.

All participation is voluntary, and public transportation will still be available to those who want to use it.

LGC Team will ask for suggestions and advice from NGOs(such as WWF, who implemented "The earth hour"). In order to have more tools to guide the team on how to develop the project. 
 
Once LCG Team recieves feedback from NGOs.They will present the formal proposal to the Institute of Technology of Buenos Aires (ITBA) and to the MIT Colab
 
The team is looking forward to receive feedback from CLIMATE COLAB in order to improve the project. ITBA will give their consent and support to it.
 
We received the recognition and prize from MIT Colab thanks to the backing through votes from ITBA.
 
LGC Team will contact several organizations to help with fundraising and to add value to the final event (GOOGLE, MASS MEDIA, SPONSORS, etc). Nevertheless to make it happen we need firstly the GOVERNMENT authorization.
 
The government will notificate the companies of the proposal and ask for their cooperation by allowing the employees to work from home. They will also have to arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes.
 
Distance walked and GHG savings: To have an estimation of the GHGs saved is necessary an app (develop by GOOGLE if possible) that will measure them.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page.But it is important to keep record of the route like Google Maps does. 
 
Once the proposal is accepted the next step is to settle a date and communicate it to the society, a key actor in this process is MASS MEDIA. 
 
The day of the event SPONSORS will play an important role by motivating the population to walk more. This could be achived by offering refreshments and prizes. 
 
Finally,all the information is collected by GOOGLE. Including steps per person and the equivalence with the reduction of GHGs obteined.
 
The LGC Team will analyze the results and make papers to be published and to communicate it to the community, the government and the MIT. 
 
As the results will be positive this idea can be implemented in other countries, all over the world. By this the proposal will reach a greater impact and a change in people's habits worldwide.


Who will take these actions?
LGC Team at ITBA: designs proposal and presents it to Climate Colab.
 
Argentine Government sponsors "LET'S WALK day"
 
Media covers the events publishing metrics on energy saved, emissions saved and money saved.
 
5 % local city population participates in the "LET'S WALK day"
 
NGOs sponsor event.
 
Transportation works as usual.

Other countries can implement the proposal to have a higher impact
Where will these actions be taken?
City level: The first objective is to do it in Buenos Aires City as is the most populated region, the most contaminating one and the principal concentration of companies.

Country level: Later by the positive results our goal is to carry out the experience at country level, in cities like Cordoba, Rosario, Mendoza and San Luis.

Worldwide: Finally, other countries can use the proposal to make their own impacts since everything counts.

 

In addition, specify the country or countries where these actions will be taken.
Argentina

Country 2
Brazil

Country 3
Chile

Country 4
United States

Country 5
No country selected

Impact/Benefits
What impact will these actions have on greenhouse gas emissions and/or adapting to climate change?
1) City of Buenos Aires

a) Short term CO2e emissions saved in Buenos Aires city:

We postulate that 5 % of the population will participate in the event.
Public transportation use: 80% of population [1]

 
Emissions saved by cars commuting from Buenos Aires Province:

10410352 cars/month [2] /30 days/month x 15 km/trip  x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] x 0.05 ( 5 % population participates  in event) = 26 tCO2e (metric tons) / day.
 

Emission saved by cars inside the city:

2.89 million people x 5% people participation x 20 % people use cars x 7 km average trip to work x 2 trips /day x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] = 40.46 tCO2e / day
 

Total short term CO2e savings = (26 + 40.46) = 66.46 tCO2e /day

b) Long term CHG savings (5 years) in Buenos Aires city:

We forecast that recycling could increase by 10 %. Today only 23% of Buenos Aires city recycle [4]. And recycling saves from 0.34 to 10 Tonnes of CO2e from each ton of recycled material [5]. Considering an average 1 Tonne of CO2e saved per Tonne of recycled material, current Buenos Aires savings from recycling would be 976 Tonnes of domiciliary residues [6] x 23% people recycle x 0.1 (10 % recycling increase ) x 1 tCO2e/1 t recycled material = 22 Tonnes of CO2e / year saved.
 
Energy savings recommendations [7] use increment by 10 %. The average electricity consumption per electrified household in Argentina is 3600 kWh [11], so we estimate that the savings will be of 360 kWh/home per year * 963 thousand homes * 0.05 (5 % of participation) = 17334 kWh per year. This could save up to 17334 kWh x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 10.2 tCO2e/year
Distributed solar photo voltaic electricity law is approved and covers 1 % of city demand.
2.89 million people x 4100 kWh/person [10] x 0.01 (1% of demand) x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 70205 tCO2e/year
 
It incentivates the use of public transport in this way, so the Government must make it more efficient.For example, the use of electric buses is implemented in 2 % of city.[8] [9] Each bus will save 175 metrics tones of pollutant emissions and 22.700 liters of fuel per unit and year. We estimate that there are 10200 buses in Buenos Aires,so 2% is 204, the total reduction will be 204*175 mtones= 35700 metric tones of pollutant / year saved.[10]
 
Total emission savings = (22 + 10.2 + 70205 + 35700) = 105937 tCO2e/year

2) Extrapolation to world:

a) Short term:

66.46 tCO2e/(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 24997 tCO2e

b) Long term:

105.937 tCO2e/year /(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 39 million tCO2e / year.

The transportation sector accounts from 19 % to 25 % of total GHGs emissions, according to [12] and [13]

So the total GHG due to the transportation sector may be calculated as

46.000 million tCO2e x 22% = 10.120 million tCO2e

So the proposal savings would be 39 / 10120 = 0.38 % of the transportation sector emissions.

What are other key benefits?
Generate awareness on using more public transport
Government will have to improve the efficiency of public transport
Change the habits of the society towards a sustainable and ecological life
Smog and sound pollution will be reduced
People´s stress levels will be reduced
Increase the use of bicycles
Change habits and weaken sedentary lifestyle
Cut costs of transport and so combustibles
By implemeting home-office besides helping this cause, the employeers will have a cut down of their costs
Decrease GHGs emissions by reducing the use of cars and public transport
Costs/Challenges
What are the proposal’s projected costs?
1) Cost of security forces involved in organizing the event.

2) Cost of health support personnel, ambulances, paramedics,

3) Cost of traditional media ads

4) Cost of blog, SN ads and  social media managers / influencers

5) Cost of winner prizes

6) Cost of collaboration agreement with smartphone apps that calculate distances walked by people participating in the event

7) Cost of webpage that summarizes energy savings, money saved, GHGs emission saved.

Timeline
The day of the event, there will be an immediate impact by the reduction of the GHGs emissions.
In the short-term we are looking forward to make people realize that their can make a huge change on a daily basis.For example, they can avoid the use of cars in short distances.
Over more, we can affirm that one of important change will be the use bikes. Nowadays, Buenos Aires city provides free bikes and bicicles in the downtown. Our idea is to massify these kind of habits and became a green country free of sedentarism.
What's more, we want to Incentive companies to stablish a home-office day. This will allow people who live far from their jobs to colaborate to this cause.
Medium-term: These "Homeoffice days" could at first be once a year, but once people catch the habit, it could be done monthly.
Long-term: Eventually people could do these "Homeoffice days" weekly, so that people enjoy life a little more and savings of energy, money and GHGs emissions are multiplied.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'perfect storm' of unverified assumptions: 5% of the city's population voluntarily walking/biking to work on a single day, simultaneous government authorization for mass home-office, and the development of a custom tracking app by a major tech giant (Google) without a formal partnership. The causal chain assumes that a one-day event will trigger complex systemic changes (e.g., 2% of bus fleet electrification, 1% solar adoption) which are policy and infrastructure decisions, not behavioral outcomes of a single awareness day. The logistical coordination required for security, health support, and media coverage for a spontaneous mass movement is underestimated.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day—is a well-established global practice (e.g., Bike to Work Day, Earth Hour). The proposal explicitly cites existing programs in the US and Canada. The only novel element is the specific aggregation of data via a smartphone app to calculate GHG savings, but the proposal admits to using existing algorithms and suggests relying on Google to build the tool, which is not a technical innovation but a resource request. The 'business model' is a standard sponsorship/awareness campaign.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact calculation (66.46 tCO2e/day) is mathematically plausible *if* the participation assumptions hold, but the long-term impact projections are scientifically unsound. The proposal claims a single event will cause a 10% increase in recycling, a 1% city-wide solar adoption, and a 2% electric bus fleet conversion. These are structural, capital-intensive, and legislative outcomes that cannot be causally attributed to a one-day walking event. The extrapolation to '39 million tCO2e/year' globally is a gross overestimation based on linear scaling of a one-day behavioral anomaly, ignoring the rebound effect and the lack of infrastructure to sustain such behavior.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the presentation of evidence is flawed. It mixes short-term behavioral metrics with long-term infrastructure policy goals without distinguishing the causal mechanisms. The reliance on image placeholders (e.g., 'Main value generation loops v1.0.png') and the citation of a 'Government web page' without a specific, verifiable link reduces technical credibility. The tone is optimistic but lacks the rigorous uncertainty analysis required for scientific evaluation.

A1.perspective_summary: From a systems and empirical evidence perspective, this proposal conflates a one-day awareness campaign with long-term structural decarbonization. While the immediate behavioral shift (walking instead of driving) is plausible, the causal claims linking this event to massive infrastructure changes (solar laws, electric bus fleets) and global scaling are scientifically unsupported. The proposal treats complex socio-technical systems as linear inputs where a single event triggers disproportionate, permanent outputs.
key_concerns: Causal Fallacy: The proposal assumes a one-day event can causally drive multi-year infrastructure projects (solar law approval, bus electrification) and legislative changes. There is no mechanism described for how a 'walk day' forces these systemic shifts.

Data Integrity: The proposal relies on a custom app developed by Google (a major assumption) and uses a generic 'ecological footprint' algorithm. Without a validated, peer-reviewed methodology for converting steps to CO2e savings specific to the local grid and transport mix, the reported metrics are scientifically weak.

Participation Assumption: The 5% participation rate for a voluntary, non-mandatory event in a dense city like Buenos Aires is an optimistic upper bound. If actual participation is 0.5%, the calculated impact drops by an order of magnitude.

Rebound Effect: The proposal ignores the 'rebound effect' where participants might drive more on other days or where the event itself generates emissions (media travel, event logistics) that offset the savings.

Scalability: Extrapolating a one-day event in one city to a global annual saving of 39 million tons of CO2e is mathematically invalid. It assumes 100% adoption of the behavior globally without accounting for cultural, infrastructural, and climatic constraints.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'magic wand' assumption of government and corporate cooperation without a defined regulatory or political pathway. It demands the government 'arrange with transportation about the modification of their routes' and 'notify companies' to allow home office, yet offers no mechanism for enforcement or incentive alignment. The reliance on a custom Google-developed app and the assumption that 5% of the population will voluntarily participate in a single-day event without a robust mobilization strategy renders the operational feasibility low. Furthermore, the proposal conflates a one-day awareness event with long-term structural policy changes (e.g., passing a solar law or implementing electric buses), which are outside the scope of a single event's governance.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day—is a well-established global practice (referenced in the text itself regarding US/Canada programs). The proposal adds little institutional novelty; it attempts to repackage existing awareness campaigns with a 'Big Data' layer. The suggestion to use an app to track walking is standard in the current digital health landscape, not a governance innovation. The novelty is superficial, relying on the aggregation of existing ideas rather than a new regulatory or institutional approach to climate action.

impact.rationale: The quantitative impact claims are mathematically flawed and politically unrealistic. The proposal extrapolates a one-day event's short-term savings into a 5-year long-term impact model that assumes the event will single-handedly drive a 10% increase in recycling, the approval of a solar law, and the implementation of electric buses. These are legislative and infrastructural outcomes that cannot be causally linked to a single awareness day. The calculated 0.38% of global transportation emissions reduction is based on a flawed extrapolation of a single-day event to a global scale, ignoring the transient nature of behavioral change without sustained policy support.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with standard sections (Business Model Canvas, Impact/Benefits), but the internal logic is inconsistent. It oscillates between describing a 'practice' and a 'project,' and the narrative jumps from a single-day event to multi-year policy achievements without explaining the governance bridge. The inclusion of broken image references (e.g., 'main-objections.png') and the reliance on external links for core arguments weakens the self-containment of the document. While the intent is clear, the presentation fails to rigorously defend the causal links between the event and the massive long-term impacts claimed.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance and regulatory perspective, this proposal is a classic case of 'awareness over action.' It mistakes a public relations event for a policy intervention. While the intent to promote walking and reduce emissions is positive, the proposal lacks the institutional levers, political strategy, and regulatory realism required to achieve its stated long-term goals. It assumes that a single day of voluntary participation can trigger complex legislative and infrastructural changes, which is a fundamental misunderstanding of how public policy and behavioral change are governed.
key_concerns: The proposal assumes government and corporate entities will voluntarily alter operations (routes, home office policies) based on a single event without a formal regulatory framework or incentive structure.
The causal link between a one-day 'Walk Day' and long-term legislative outcomes (solar law approval, electric bus implementation) is logically unsound and lacks a governance mechanism.

The data collection strategy relies on a hypothetical 'Google-developed app' and voluntary participation, raising issues of data privacy, accuracy, and scalability without a clear legal basis for data usage.
The extrapolation of short-term, one-day emission savings to global, long-term impact figures is mathematically and methodologically flawed, inflating the perceived impact.
There is no risk assessment regarding public safety, traffic management, or the potential for the event to disrupt essential services, which are critical for government approval.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'big bang' implementation model requiring simultaneous government authorization, corporate policy shifts (mandatory home office), and mass media coordination for a single day event. The assumption that the government will 'arrange with transportation about the modification of their routes' for a one-day event is operationally unrealistic; transit agencies do not re-route based on ad-hoc awareness days. Furthermore, the reliance on a custom app developed by 'Google if possible' without a defined technical partner or budget creates a critical execution bottleneck. The logistical burden of security and health support for a city-wide walking event is underestimated.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days—is a mature, globally standardized practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Month). The proposal adds little novelty beyond the specific context of Buenos Aires. The proposed 'value generation loop' of using big data to inform transit planning is a standard feature of existing mobility apps (e.g., Citymapper, Google Maps) and does not require a new, single-day event to achieve. The 'altruistic spirit' is a motivation, not an innovative mechanism.

impact.rationale: The quantitative impact claims are mathematically flawed and operationally disconnected. The proposal calculates long-term impacts (e.g., 35,700 tons of CO2 saved from electric buses) as a direct result of a one-day walking event, which is a non-sequitur; a single day of walking does not trigger the procurement and deployment of electric bus fleets. The extrapolation to '39 million tCO2e/year' globally based on a 5% participation rate in one city ignores the massive variance in urban density, infrastructure, and culture. The actual direct impact is limited to the emissions avoided on that single day (approx. 66 tons), which is negligible on a climate scale.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structurally fragmented, mixing a one-day event with a 5-year policy roadmap without distinguishing between the two. The Business Model Canvas references are present but the text lacks a coherent narrative on *who* pays for the implementation costs (security, ambulances, app dev) versus who benefits. The tone is overly optimistic ('we expect to have support') without addressing the high transaction costs of securing government and corporate buy-in. The reliance on image placeholders (e.g., 'main-objections.png') without textual explanation weakens the argument.

A3.perspective_summary: From an implementation standpoint, this proposal is a classic 'awareness event' masquerading as a systemic solution. It fails to account for the high friction of coordinating city-wide logistics, the lack of a sustainable funding model for the app and event costs, and the logical leap between a one-day behavioral nudge and long-term infrastructure investment. It is operationally fragile and risks being a 'one-off' with zero scalability or lasting habit formation without a much more robust, phased rollout strategy.
key_concerns: Who funds the app development and the event logistics (security, medical, prizes)? The proposal assumes 'altruistic spirit' will cover these hard costs.
The assumption that a one-day event can trigger long-term policy changes (electric bus rollout, solar laws) is a logical fallacy; these require multi-year capital budgets and legislative processes.
The proposal assumes the government will modify transit routes for a single day, which is operationally infeasible for major transit authorities.
There is no clear incentive structure for corporations to allow 'home office' on a specific day; this requires a cultural shift that a single event cannot force.
The data collection method (custom app) is a high-risk adoption barrier compared to leveraging existing platforms (Strava, Google Fit) to reduce friction.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'magic wand' assumption that the government will instantly re-route public transport and force companies to allow home office for a single day without a pilot or legal framework. The requirement for a custom Google-developed app to track specific GHG savings via route mapping is technically and financially unrealistic for a volunteer-led event. Furthermore, the plan to have 5% of the population (approx. 145,000 people) walk to work simultaneously ignores the reality of Buenos Aires' infrastructure: many workers live in peripheral areas (Conurbano) where walking 10-15 blocks is impossible due to distance and safety, making the 'skip the bus' directive dangerous or impractical for the most vulnerable populations.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day—is a well-established global practice (referenced in the proposal itself regarding US/Canada). The proposal adds little novelty beyond a specific app tracking mechanism, which is a common feature in existing fitness and carbon-tracking apps. The 'business model' of selling big data to sponsors is also a standard marketing tactic, not an innovative approach to climate action.

impact.rationale: The projected impact is inflated by a fundamental misunderstanding of the target demographic. The proposal assumes 5% of the *total* population can walk to work, ignoring that a vast portion of Buenos Aires' workforce commutes from the Greater Buenos Aires (Conurbano) where distances are 20-40km, making walking impossible. The 'skip the bus' instruction is particularly harmful; for low-income residents, public transport is a necessity, not a luxury to be skipped. The long-term impact claims (e.g., 10% increase in recycling, 5% solar coverage) are causally unconnected to a single day of walking and represent wishful thinking rather than a realistic outcome of this specific intervention.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the data is often misapplied or based on flawed assumptions (e.g., calculating global impact by simply multiplying Buenos Aires' potential by world population without accounting for urban density differences). The tone is optimistic but lacks the grounded reality of urban logistics. The inclusion of 'Business Model Canvas' and 'Objections' sections shows an attempt at rigor, but the content within them fails to address the actual barriers faced by the working class in the proposed city.

A4.perspective_summary: From the ground, this proposal feels like a top-down exercise that ignores the daily survival realities of the city's working class. It asks people to 'skip the bus' and walk to work, a directive that is physically impossible for those living in the periphery and economically dangerous for those who cannot afford to miss work or pay for alternative transport. The reliance on a high-tech app and government mandates for a one-day event creates a barrier to entry that excludes the very people most affected by climate change and pollution. It treats the city as a uniform grid rather than a complex, unequal urban fabric.
key_concerns: The instruction to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway' is dangerous and exclusionary for low-income workers who rely on public transit for long commutes; it assumes everyone lives within walking distance of their job.

The proposal assumes a 5% participation rate of the *total* population, failing to distinguish between the city center and the sprawling, car-dependent periphery (Conurbano) where walking is not a viable option.
The long-term impact projections (recycling rates, solar adoption) are causally disconnected from a single-day walking event and lack a credible mechanism for behavioral change beyond the event day.
The feasibility of a custom Google-developed app and the immediate government re-routing of public transport for a single day is highly unrealistic and ignores bureaucratic and technical constraints.
The 'Big Data' monetization model raises privacy concerns for participants, particularly if the data is used to profile vulnerable populations without their full understanding or consent.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'magic wand' assumption that the government will mandate home office, re-route public transport, and provide security/health support for a single-day event without a clear implementation pathway. The reliance on Google to build a custom app and the assumption that 5% of the population will voluntarily participate in a coordinated global-scale behavioral shift on a single day ignores the massive logistical friction and the lack of a concrete governance mechanism to enforce the necessary corporate and municipal cooperation.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days—is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day). The proposal adds a data-tracking layer, but the underlying value proposition is not novel. It repackages existing awareness campaigns without introducing a new mechanism for behavioral change or a distinct technological innovation beyond standard footprint calculators.

impact.rationale: The proposal hides a severe distributional injustice: it assumes universal mobility. By demanding people 'skip the bus' and 'turn to their own bodies,' it implicitly excludes low-income workers who rely on public transit for long commutes, people with disabilities, and those living in food deserts where walking to groceries is unsafe or impossible. Furthermore, the long-term impact calculations are mathematically incoherent; attributing 70,000+ tonnes of CO2 savings to a 'law' that covers 1% of demand, or assuming a one-day walk event will trigger a 10% permanent increase in recycling, represents a fundamental misunderstanding of causal mechanisms and policy efficacy.

presentation.rationale: The presentation is structurally weak and normatively opaque. It presents speculative long-term policy outcomes (solar law approval, electric bus implementation) as direct consequences of a one-day walking event, obscuring the actual causal chain. The text contains significant grammatical errors and logical leaps (e.g., 'skip the bus' to reduce emissions, when buses are low-carbon compared to cars), failing to address the trade-offs of excluding public transit users or the feasibility of the proposed 'big data' collection without privacy safeguards.

A5.perspective_summary: This proposal masks a 'one-size-fits-all' approach under the guise of altruism, ignoring the structural barriers that prevent equitable participation. It conflates a single-day awareness stunt with systemic policy change, making inflated claims about long-term impacts that lack causal grounding. The exclusion of those who cannot walk or bike due to distance, disability, or economic necessity is a critical ethical failure that the proposal fails to address.
key_concerns: Exclusion Risk: The proposal explicitly tells people to 'skip the bus' and 'turn to their own bodies,' which effectively excludes low-income commuters, people with disabilities, and those with long commutes, reinforcing mobility inequality.

Causal Fallacy: The proposal attributes massive long-term policy wins (solar law, electric bus rollout) to a one-day walking event, creating a false narrative of impact that obscures the need for actual legislative and infrastructure work.

Data Privacy & Surveillance: The plan to collect 'big data' on individual movement routes via a Google app without a clear privacy framework or consent mechanism poses significant risks to participant anonymity.

Feasibility of Mandates: The proposal assumes the government can and will mandate home office days and re-route public transport for a single event, which is politically and logistically unrealistic without a detailed stakeholder engagement strategy.

False Neutrality on Transport: By framing public transport as something to be 'skipped' to save emissions, the proposal ignores that buses are a low-carbon alternative to cars, potentially discouraging a shift from private cars to public transit.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a fragile chain of dependencies: government authorization, corporate cooperation for home office, and the development of a custom app by a major tech giant (Google) without a clear technical or business case for the latter. The assumption that a single-day event will trigger systemic changes like the approval of a 'Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law' or a 2% shift to electric buses is operationally unrealistic; these require legislative and capital cycles far beyond the scope of an awareness day.

novelty.rationale: The concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day). The proposal adds a smartphone tracking layer, but this is a standard gamification tactic, not a novel systemic intervention. The core mechanism—temporary behavioral substitution for a single day—lacks innovation in addressing the structural drivers of urban mobility.

impact.rationale: The long-term impact claims are severely inflated and logically disconnected from the intervention. Extrapolating a one-day event to a 0.38% reduction in global transportation emissions (39 million tCO2e/year) is a fundamental systems error; it assumes a permanent, linear scaling of a temporary behavioral shift without accounting for rebound effects or habit decay. The proposal confuses 'awareness' with 'structural resilience,' failing to demonstrate how a single day of walking alters the entrenched infrastructure or policy frameworks required for genuine long-term decarbonization.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts quantitative analysis, which is commendable. However, the presentation is undermined by the inclusion of highly speculative and mathematically dubious long-term forecasts (e.g., linking a walking day to solar law approval) that weaken the credibility of the entire argument. The reliance on 'big data' from a hypothetical Google app without a defined data governance or privacy strategy is also a presentation gap.

A6.perspective_summary: From a long-term sustainability lens, this proposal is a classic case of 'symbolic action' masquerading as systemic change. While the immediate health and awareness benefits of a 'Walk Day' are positive, the proposal fails to address the resilience of the urban mobility system. It assumes that a temporary reduction in car use will spontaneously trigger complex legislative and infrastructural shifts (solar laws, electric bus fleets) that typically require decades of advocacy and capital investment. The projected long-term impacts are mathematically unsound, treating a transient behavioral nudge as a permanent structural lever.
key_concerns: The causal link between a one-day awareness event and the approval of major legislation (solar law) or infrastructure shifts (electric buses) is non-existent; this represents a dangerous overestimation of behavioral spillover effects.

The 'long-term impact' calculations (39 million tCO2e/year) rely on the false assumption that a single day's behavior change will be sustained indefinitely and scaled globally without friction, ignoring habit decay and the rebound effect.

The proposal shifts the burden of systemic change onto individual voluntary action without addressing the structural barriers (urban sprawl, lack of safe bike lanes, rigid work cultures) that prevent walking/biking from being a viable daily option.
Dependency on a 'Google-developed app' is a critical single point of failure; the proposal lacks a realistic path to technical implementation or data ownership.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal rests on a fragile causal chain requiring simultaneous, uncoordinated actions from government (route modification), corporations (mandatory home office), and a specific tech giant (custom app development). As peers A2 and A3 correctly noted, the assumption that a single-day event can trigger complex logistical changes (e.g., transit re-routing) or legislative outcomes (solar laws) is empirically unsupported. The reliance on a 'Google-developed app' without a defined technical partner or budget represents a critical single point of failure, rendering the operational feasibility low.

novelty.rationale: The core mechanism—a 'Walk/Bike to Work' day—is a mature, globally standardized intervention (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Month). The proposal's attempt to layer 'Big Data' collection via a custom app does not constitute a novel scientific or systems innovation; similar data aggregation is already standard in existing mobility platforms (e.g., Strava, Citymapper). The novelty is superficial, repackaging existing awareness campaigns without introducing a new causal mechanism for behavioral change or emission reduction.

impact.rationale: The quantitative impact claims suffer from severe methodological flaws. The proposal conflates a one-day transient event with long-term structural policy outcomes (e.g., attributing the approval of a solar law or the deployment of electric buses to a single walking day). The extrapolation of 66 tCO2e/day to a global annual figure of 39 million tCO2e ignores the non-linear nature of behavioral adoption and the lack of sustained policy drivers. The direct, measurable impact is limited to the emissions avoided on the specific day, which is negligible on a climate scale.

presentation.rationale: The proposal utilizes standard structural elements (Business Model Canvas, Impact sections), but the internal logic is inconsistent, oscillating between a 'practice' and a 'project' without a clear governance bridge. The reliance on broken image placeholders and external links for core arguments weakens the document's self-containment. While the intent is clear, the presentation fails to rigorously defend the causal links between the event and the massive long-term impacts claimed, a weakness highlighted by peers A2 and A3.

A1.perspective_summary: From a systems and empirical evidence perspective, this proposal exhibits a fundamental category error: it treats a public relations awareness event as a systemic policy intervention. The causal mechanisms linking a single day of voluntary walking to long-term legislative changes (solar laws, electric bus fleets) are non-existent. The proposal relies on 'magic wand' assumptions regarding government and corporate cooperation without defining the regulatory or incentive structures required to make such cooperation plausible. The scientific uncertainty regarding the scalability of this behavioral nudge is high, and the projected impact figures are mathematically unsound.
key_concerns: The causal link between a one-day event and long-term legislative/infrastructural outcomes (solar law, electric buses) is logically unsound and lacks a governance mechanism.

The proposal assumes government and corporate entities will voluntarily alter operations (routes, home office policies) based on a single event without a formal regulatory framework or incentive structure.

The data collection strategy relies on a hypothetical 'Google-developed app' and voluntary participation, raising issues of data privacy, accuracy, and scalability without a clear legal basis for data usage.
The extrapolation of short-term, one-day emission savings to global, long-term impact figures is mathematically and methodologically flawed, inflating the perceived impact.
There is no risk assessment regarding public safety, traffic management, or the potential for the event to disrupt essential services, which are critical for government approval.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal is institutionally non-viable in its current form. It relies on a 'perfect storm' of unverified assumptions: the Argentine government unilaterally mandating or coordinating mass home-office and transit route modifications for a single day, and Google developing a custom tracking app without a formal contract or budget. As A1 and A3 correctly noted, transit agencies do not re-route based on ad-hoc awareness days, and the assumption that a one-day event can trigger multi-year legislative outcomes (solar laws, bus electrification) represents a fundamental failure of governance logic. The proposal lacks a realistic implementation pathway, budget, or legal framework for the required public-private coordination.

novelty.rationale: The core mechanism is a standard 'Walk/Bike to Work' day, a mature global practice. The proposed 'innovation'—using an app to track steps and calculate GHG savings—adds no novel governance or technical value, as this data is already aggregated by existing platforms (Google Maps, Strava, Citymapper). The proposal attempts to repackage a known behavioral nudge as a systemic solution, but the underlying mechanism remains a low-complexity awareness campaign with no unique institutional leverage.

impact.rationale: The impact projections are scientifically and politically unsound. The proposal conflates a one-day behavioral anomaly with long-term structural decarbonization, claiming a single event will cause a 2% electric bus fleet conversion and 1% solar adoption. These are capital-intensive, legislative outcomes that cannot be causally attributed to a 'walk day.' The extrapolation to '39 million tCO2e/year' globally is a gross overestimation that ignores the lack of infrastructure, cultural variance, and the 'rebound effect.' The actual direct impact is limited to the emissions avoided on that single day (approx. 66 tons), which is negligible on a climate scale and fails to justify the massive coordination costs.

presentation.rationale: The presentation is structurally fragmented and lacks the rigor required for policy evaluation. It mixes short-term behavioral metrics with long-term infrastructure policy goals without distinguishing causal mechanisms. The reliance on image placeholders and the citation of a generic 'Government web page' without specific, verifiable links reduces credibility. The tone is overly optimistic, assuming 'altruistic spirit' will cover hard costs (security, medical, app development) without addressing the high transaction costs of securing government and corporate buy-in.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance and regulatory feasibility lens, this proposal is a classic case of 'policy wishful thinking.' It attempts to solve complex, capital-intensive structural problems (transport electrification, energy law) through a low-cost, one-day behavioral nudge. The institutional levers required (government mandate on home-office, transit re-routing, tech giant app development) are either non-existent or politically impossible to secure for a single event. The proposal fails to distinguish between an awareness campaign and a policy intervention, rendering it unimplementable as described.
key_concerns: Institutional Overreach: The proposal assumes the government will modify transit routes and mandate home-office for a single day, which is operationally and politically infeasible.
Causal Fallacy: There is no logical mechanism linking a one-day walking event to the approval of solar laws or the procurement of electric bus fleets.
Funding Vacuum: No clear funding model exists for the app development, security, medical support, and prizes; reliance on 'altruism' is not a viable budget strategy.
Data Integrity: The proposal relies on a custom app developed by Google without a formal partnership, creating a critical execution bottleneck.
Scalability Myth: Extrapolating a one-day event in one city to global annual savings ignores the massive variance in urban density, infrastructure, and culture.

feasibility.rationale: I stand by the low feasibility score. The proposal's operational model collapses under the weight of its dependencies: it requires a 'perfect storm' of voluntary corporate home-office mandates, government route modifications, and a custom app built by Google without a signed contract. As peers A1 and A2 noted, the assumption that a one-day event can trigger complex infrastructure changes (solar laws, bus electrification) is not just optimistic; it is operationally impossible. The lack of a defined budget for the 'security forces' and 'health support' mentioned in the cost section further highlights a disconnect from the reality of managing a mass urban event. The reliance on a single-day behavioral shift to drive multi-year policy outcomes is a fundamental implementation flaw.

novelty.rationale: The peers correctly identified that 'Bike to Work' and 'Walk to Work' days are mature, global practices. The proposal's attempt to layer a 'Big Data' app on top of this is not a novel innovation but a standard marketing tactic. The suggestion to use an existing 'ecological footprint' algorithm and ask Google to build the tool adds no technical novelty. The 'Business Model Canvas' presented is a generic template filled with aspirational sponsorships rather than a novel revenue or value-generation mechanism. There is no new technology, process, or institutional model introduced here.

impact.rationale: While the short-term math (66 tCO2e) is internally consistent *if* the 5% participation rate is met, the long-term impact claims are scientifically and operationally bankrupt. The proposal claims a single event will cause a 10% rise in recycling, the passage of a solar law, and the deployment of electric buses. These are capital-intensive, legislative, and infrastructural outcomes that cannot be causally attributed to a one-day awareness campaign. The extrapolation to '39 million tCO2e/year' globally is a gross overestimation that ignores the rebound effect and the lack of infrastructure to sustain such behavior. The impact is real only for the single day, and even then, it is likely overestimated due to the lack of a control group or accounting for event logistics emissions.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with standard sections, but the internal logic is fractured. It oscillates between a 'practice' and a 'project' and fails to distinguish between a one-day event and long-term policy goals. The reliance on image placeholders (e.g., 'Main value generation loops v1.0.png') and broken links reduces credibility. However, the attempt to quantify impact, even if flawed, shows an effort to be data-driven, which keeps the score from dropping to 2. The presentation is clear in its intent but weak in its evidentiary support.

A3.perspective_summary: From an implementation and operational feasibility lens, this proposal is a classic 'awareness trap.' It mistakes a public relations event for a scalable climate solution. The operational requirements (government route changes, corporate home-office mandates, custom app development) are treated as trivial assumptions rather than high-risk barriers. The causal chain linking a one-day walk to multi-year infrastructure projects is non-existent. While the intent to promote walking is positive, the proposal lacks the operational rigor, resource planning, and realistic impact modeling required for actual deployment. It is a concept that looks good on paper but fails the 'how do we actually do this?' test.
key_concerns: Operational Dependency: The proposal assumes Google will build a custom app and the government will modify bus routes without a formal agreement or budget. This is a critical implementation risk.
Causal Fallacy: There is no mechanism described for how a one-day event triggers long-term legislative changes (solar law, bus electrification). This is a fundamental flaw in the impact model.
Resource Gap: The cost section lists 'security forces' and 'health support' but provides no budget estimates or funding sources, making the event financially unfeasible.
Data Integrity: The reliance on a hypothetical app and voluntary participation for data collection introduces significant accuracy and privacy risks.
Scalability: The global extrapolation ignores cultural, infrastructural, and climatic constraints, rendering the long-term impact figures meaningless.

feasibility.rationale: I stand by the low score. The proposal's reliance on a 'Google-developed app' and government mandates for home office is not just logistically difficult; it is socially disconnected from the reality of Buenos Aires. As A5 and A6 noted, the assumption that a single-day event can trigger complex legislative changes (solar laws, electric bus fleets) is a fantasy. From the ground, this looks like a top-down directive that ignores the daily struggle of workers who cannot simply 'skip the bus' or work from home. The lack of a concrete pathway for the 'big data' collection and the unrealistic expectation of corporate compliance make this unfeasible.

novelty.rationale: The peers are correct; this is a repackaging of 'Bike to Work' and 'Earth Hour' concepts. The addition of a tracking app does not constitute a novel mechanism for behavioral change. It is a standard gamification tactic applied to a well-worn idea. There is no new social technology or community engagement model proposed here that addresses the specific frictions of the local context.

impact.rationale: I am lowering my score from a tentative 2 to a 1 based on the peers' rigorous dismantling of the impact claims. A5 and A6 correctly identified that the proposal's impact calculations are not just optimistic, but mathematically incoherent and ethically dangerous. The proposal explicitly tells people to 'skip the bus' and 'turn to their own bodies,' which is a direct attack on the mobility of low-income residents, people with disabilities, and those with long commutes. By framing public transit as something to be avoided, the proposal actively harms the most vulnerable populations while claiming to help the planet. The attribution of massive long-term policy wins (like the solar law) to a one-day walk is a causal fallacy that obscures the real work needed. The 'impact' is not just low; it is potentially negative due to the exclusionary nature of the advice.

presentation.rationale: The presentation fails to address the social frictions it creates. It presents speculative policy outcomes as direct consequences of a walking event, which is misleading. The text contains logical leaps (e.g., 'skip the bus' to save emissions) that ignore the reality that buses are a low-carbon alternative to cars. The lack of a privacy framework for the proposed 'big data' collection and the grammatical errors further undermine its credibility. It presents a 'one-size-fits-all' solution that does not fit the diverse realities of the city.

A4.perspective_summary: This proposal is a classic example of 'symbolic action' that ignores the lived reality of the community. It assumes a level of mobility and flexibility that does not exist for many residents, effectively excluding low-income workers, people with disabilities, and those with long commutes. The proposal's impact claims are mathematically unsound and ethically flawed, as they attribute systemic policy changes to a single-day event while actively discouraging the use of public transit. The 'big data' collection plan lacks a privacy framework, and the reliance on a 'Google app' is a critical single point of failure. This is not a sustainable innovation; it is a well-intentioned but deeply flawed idea that could exacerbate existing inequalities.
key_concerns: Exclusionary Advice: The directive to 'skip the bus' and 'turn to your own bodies' is a direct affront to the mobility needs of low-income workers, people with disabilities, and those with long commutes, reinforcing social inequality.

Causal Fallacy: The proposal falsely attributes massive long-term policy wins (solar law, electric bus rollout) to a one-day walking event, creating a misleading narrative that obscures the need for actual legislative and infrastructure work.

Data Privacy & Surveillance: The plan to collect 'big data' on individual movement routes via a Google app without a clear privacy framework or consent mechanism poses significant risks to participant anonymity and trust.

Feasibility of Mandates: The assumption that the government can and will mandate home office days and re-route public transport for a single event is politically and logistically unrealistic without a detailed stakeholder engagement strategy.

False Neutrality on Transport: By framing public transport as something to be 'skipped' to save emissions, the proposal ignores that buses are a low-carbon alternative to cars, potentially discouraging a shift from private cars to public transit.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal's feasibility collapses under the weight of its exclusionary assumptions. As peers A4 and A6 correctly identified, the directive to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway' is not merely impractical; it is structurally impossible for the working class in the Conurbano (periphery) who rely on these systems for survival. The assumption that 5% of the total population can walk to work ignores the spatial inequality of Buenos Aires, where distance is a function of class. Furthermore, the reliance on a 'Google-developed app' and immediate government re-routing of transit for a single day represents a 'magic wand' dependency that ignores bureaucratic inertia and the lack of a viable business case for the tech giant. The plan fails to account for the cost of safety and security for vulnerable populations walking in unsafe areas.

novelty.rationale: The proposal offers no novel mechanism for climate action. It repackages the well-established 'Bike to Work' and 'Walk to Work' campaigns (referenced in the text itself) with a superficial layer of 'Big Data' tracking. The core value proposition—temporary behavioral substitution for a single day—is a standard, low-impact tactic. The 'business model' of selling participant data to sponsors is a common marketing practice, not an innovative approach to sustainability. There is no new normative framework or structural intervention proposed; it is a reiteration of individualistic awareness campaigns that have historically failed to shift systemic mobility patterns.

impact.rationale: The projected impact is ethically and mathematically flawed. The proposal inflates impact by assuming a single day of walking will causally trigger complex legislative outcomes (e.g., 5% solar coverage, 10% recycling increase) and systemic infrastructure shifts (electric buses). This is a 'symbolic action' fallacy. More critically, the impact calculation ignores the distributional consequences: the 'savings' are calculated based on a population that can afford to walk, while the 'costs' (safety risks, time loss, inability to work) are borne by those who cannot. The extrapolation to global impact (39 million tCO2e) is a statistical error that treats a transient, voluntary nudge as a permanent structural lever, masking the reality that the proposal likely excludes the very populations most affected by climate change.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structurally organized and attempts to use data, which provides a veneer of rigor. However, the presentation is undermined by the inclusion of highly speculative forecasts that lack causal grounding. The use of 'Business Model Canvas' and 'Objections' sections suggests an attempt at comprehensiveness, but the content within them fails to address the fundamental equity barriers (urban sprawl, safety, economic necessity) that define the reality of the target city. The tone is optimistic but lacks the critical self-reflection required to acknowledge who is being excluded by the 'voluntary' nature of the event.

A5.perspective_summary: From a justice and fairness lens, this proposal is a top-down exercise that masks deep structural inequalities under the guise of 'awareness.' It asks the most vulnerable to bear the cost of a symbolic gesture (walking long distances in unsafe areas or skipping essential transit) while promising benefits that will likely accrue to the already privileged. The 'Big Data' extraction from participants without clear governance raises significant ethical concerns regarding privacy and the commodification of vulnerable populations. The proposal fails to recognize that climate action cannot be achieved by asking the poor to walk further; it requires addressing the spatial and economic barriers that force car dependency in the first place.
key_concerns: The directive to 'skip the bus' is exclusionary and dangerous for low-income workers in the periphery who have no viable alternative to public transit.
The proposal conflates 'awareness' with 'structural change,' assuming a single day of walking can trigger complex legislative and infrastructural shifts without a credible mechanism.
The 'Big Data' monetization model risks exploiting participant data without adequate privacy protections or consent mechanisms, particularly for vulnerable groups.
The impact calculations ignore the spatial inequality of Buenos Aires, treating the city as a uniform grid rather than a landscape of unequal access and safety.
The reliance on a 'Google-developed app' and immediate government mandates creates a single point of failure that ignores the reality of bureaucratic and technical constraints.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal's feasibility collapses under systemic scrutiny. The directive to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway' is not just logistically difficult; it is a systemic failure that ignores the reality of urban sprawl (Conurbano) where walking is physically impossible for the majority of the workforce. Furthermore, the assumption that a single-day event can trigger complex, long-term legislative outcomes (solar law approval, electric bus rollout) represents a fundamental misunderstanding of policy inertia. The reliance on a 'magic wand' government mandate for home office and transport re-routing without a pilot or legal framework renders the project non-viable in its current form.

novelty.rationale: The concept is a repackaging of established 'Walk/Bike to Work' campaigns with no novel mechanism for systemic change. The proposed 'big data' tracking via a custom Google app is a standard feature in existing fitness/carbon apps, not an innovation. The proposal fails to introduce a new model for behavioral persistence or resilience, relying instead on a one-off awareness stunt that has been tried globally with limited long-term retention.

impact.rationale: The projected impact is not just inflated; it is causally incoherent and potentially regressive. The proposal attributes massive long-term policy wins (e.g., 70,000+ tonnes of CO2 savings from a solar law) to a single day of walking, a logical fallacy that obscures the actual drivers of decarbonization. More critically, the 'skip the bus' instruction actively undermines resilience by discouraging low-carbon public transit use in favor of walking, which is only feasible for a tiny, privileged subset of the population. This creates a 'resilience gap' where the most vulnerable are excluded, and the net emissions reduction is negligible compared to the systemic shifts required.

presentation.rationale: While the proposal attempts a structured format with business canvases and data tables, the content reveals a profound lack of systems thinking. The data is misapplied (e.g., extrapolating Buenos Aires density to global populations without adjustment), and the causal links between a one-day event and multi-year legislative changes are presented as facts rather than hypotheses. The presentation masks these deep structural flaws with optimistic language, failing to address the trade-offs and exclusionary nature of the proposed actions.

A6.perspective_summary: From a long-term resilience and systems perspective, this proposal is a dangerous oversimplification. It treats climate action as a series of isolated, voluntary behavioral stunts rather than a systemic transformation of infrastructure and policy. By ignoring the spatial and economic realities of the city (specifically the Conurbano), it risks reinforcing existing inequalities while offering negligible ecological benefit. The conflation of a single-day event with long-term legislative success is a critical failure of logic that undermines the proposal's credibility as a sustainability intervention.
key_concerns: Systemic Exclusion: The 'skip the bus' directive is fundamentally flawed for a city with high sprawl, effectively excluding the working class and those with long commutes, thereby reducing social resilience.

Causal Fallacy: The proposal falsely attributes long-term policy outcomes (solar law, electric buses) to a one-day awareness event, ignoring the decades-long timelines and complex stakeholder negotiations required for such changes.

Resilience Deficit: The plan does not build adaptive capacity; it relies on a fragile, one-off event that cannot sustain behavioral change or withstand systemic shocks (e.g., weather, economic downturns).
Data Privacy & Surveillance: The 'big data' collection model lacks a privacy framework, risking the exploitation of vulnerable populations' movement data without consent.
Misguided Metrics: The calculation of global impact by simply multiplying local potential ignores urban density differences and the non-linear nature of climate systems.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal is operationally non-viable. It relies on a 'perfect storm' of unverified assumptions: the Argentine government unilaterally modifying transit routes, corporations mandating home-office, and Google developing a custom app without a contract or budget. As all three agents (A1, A2, A3) agreed, the causal link between a single-day event and complex logistical or legislative changes is logically unsound. The lack of a defined budget for security, medical support, and app development renders the project impossible to execute as described.

novelty.rationale: The core mechanism is a mature, globally standardized intervention ('Walk/Bike to Work' day). The proposed 'innovation' of layering a custom app for data tracking adds no novel technical or governance value, as similar data aggregation is standard in existing platforms (Strava, Citymapper). The proposal repackages an existing awareness campaign without introducing a new causal mechanism for behavioral change.

impact.rationale: The impact projections are scientifically and methodologically flawed. The proposal conflates a one-day transient event with long-term structural policy outcomes (e.g., attributing the approval of solar laws or electric bus deployment to a single walking day). The extrapolation of short-term savings to a global annual figure of 39 million tCO2e ignores the non-linear nature of behavioral adoption and lacks sustained policy drivers. The actual measurable impact is negligible on a climate scale.

presentation.rationale: While the proposal uses standard structural elements, the internal logic is fractured, oscillating between a 'practice' and a 'project' without a clear governance bridge. The reliance on broken image placeholders and external links for core arguments weakens the document's self-containment. The tone is overly optimistic, assuming 'altruism' will cover hard costs without addressing the high transaction costs of securing necessary public-private coordination.

S1.synthesis: The cluster (A1, A2, A3) reached a near-unanimous consensus on the critical failures of this proposal, specifically regarding feasibility and impact. The primary disagreement was not on the *existence* of flaws, but on the *severity* of the feasibility score. A1 and A3 initially leaned towards a score of 2, citing the 'intent' and the theoretical possibility of the event occurring, whereas A2 argued for a 1, labeling it 'institutionally non-viable.' After rigorous deliberation, the cluster resolved this by prioritizing the operational reality: the proposal requires a 'magic wand' assumption that a single day of voluntary walking can trigger multi-year legislative changes (solar laws) and complex logistical shifts (transit re-routing) without a defined budget, legal framework, or formal partnerships. Since the causal chain is broken and the execution pathway is nonexistent, the cluster downgraded Feasibility to 1. Similarly, the Impact score was settled at 1 because the mathematical extrapolation to global figures was deemed scientifically unsound by all agents, rendering the claimed impact illusory. Novelty and Presentation scores were harmonized at 2 and 2 respectively, reflecting the consensus that the idea is a repackaged standard campaign presented with insufficient rigor.
S1.uncertainty: The only remaining uncertainty lies in the potential for the proposal to be radically re-scoped. If the proposal were stripped of its long-term legislative claims (solar laws, bus electrification) and the requirement for government-mandated route changes, focusing solely on a one-day awareness event with a realistic, self-funded budget, the feasibility and impact scores could theoretically rise. However, as currently written, the uncertainty regarding the 'altruistic spirit' overcoming the lack of a formal funding model and legal framework remains a critical, unresolved barrier to implementation.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal is deemed unfeasible due to a fundamental disconnect from the socio-spatial reality of Buenos Aires. The directive to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway' is structurally impossible for the majority of the workforce living in the Conurbano (periphery), where walking distances are prohibitive. Furthermore, the reliance on a 'Google-developed app' and immediate government mandates for home office and route re-routing represents a 'magic wand' dependency with no viable pathway for implementation. The assumption that a single-day event can trigger complex legislative changes (solar laws, electric bus fleets) is a causal fallacy that renders the project non-viable.

novelty.rationale: The proposal offers no novel mechanism for climate action. It is a repackaging of established 'Bike to Work' and 'Walk to Work' campaigns, overlaid with a superficial layer of 'Big Data' tracking. The core value proposition—temporary behavioral substitution for a single day—is a standard, low-impact tactic. The 'business model' of selling participant data to sponsors is a common marketing practice, not an innovative approach to sustainability. There is no new normative framework or structural intervention proposed.

impact.rationale: The projected impact is ethically and mathematically flawed. The proposal inflates impact by falsely attributing massive long-term policy wins (e.g., 70,000+ tonnes of CO2 savings from a solar law) to a single day of walking. More critically, the 'skip the bus' instruction actively undermines resilience by discouraging low-carbon public transit use in favor of walking, which is only feasible for a tiny, privileged subset of the population. This creates a 'resilience gap' where the most vulnerable are excluded, and the net emissions reduction is negligible compared to the systemic shifts required. The impact is potentially negative due to the exclusionary nature of the advice.

presentation.rationale: While the proposal attempts a structured format with business canvases and data tables, the content reveals a profound lack of systems thinking. The data is misapplied (e.g., extrapolating Buenos Aires density to global populations without adjustment), and the causal links between a one-day event and multi-year legislative changes are presented as facts rather than hypotheses. The presentation masks these deep structural flaws with optimistic language and contains grammatical errors, failing to address the trade-offs and exclusionary nature of the proposed actions.

S2.synthesis: The cluster (A4, A5, A6) reached a near-unanimous consensus that the proposal is fundamentally flawed, though the initial deliberation highlighted a slight divergence in the severity of the 'Impact' score. A4 and A6 initially leaned towards a score of 1, arguing the impact is potentially negative due to the exclusionary 'skip the bus' directive, while A5 initially considered a 2, acknowledging the 'symbolic' value of awareness. However, upon rigorous challenge, A5 conceded that the 'symbolic' value is negated by the active harm caused to low-income populations who rely on public transit. The 'skip the bus' instruction was identified as a critical failure that actively discourages a low-carbon alternative (buses) in favor of an inaccessible one (walking long distances), making the impact not just low, but regressive. The 'Feasibility' score was unanimously lowered to 1 after rejecting the 'Google app' and 'government mandate' assumptions as logistically impossible. The 'Novelty' score was settled at 2, acknowledging the 'Big Data' layer as a superficial addition to a well-worn concept rather than a true innovation. The final synthesis rejects the proposal's core logic: it treats climate action as a voluntary, individualistic stunt that ignores the structural inequalities of urban sprawl and the necessity of public transit.
S2.uncertainty: While the cluster agrees on the low scores, a minor unresolved tension remains regarding the potential for the 'Big Data' component to be salvaged if the 'skip the bus' directive were removed. A4 and A6 expressed concern that even with a privacy framework, the data collection model relies on a 'single point of failure' (Google) and lacks a clear governance structure. However, the consensus is that the proposal's foundational premise is too flawed to warrant a salvage attempt in its current form. The specific mathematical error in the global extrapolation (treating a transient nudge as a permanent structural lever) is universally agreed upon as a disqualifying error, leaving no ambiguity on the impact calculation's validity.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal is operationally impossible. It relies on a 'magic wand' assumption that a single-day voluntary event can trigger complex, multi-year legislative changes (solar laws, bus electrification) and force government/corporate mandates (home office, route re-routing) without a budget, legal framework, or formal partnerships. The requirement for Google to develop a custom app and the directive to 'skip the bus' (which is structurally impossible for the majority of the workforce in the Conurbano) render the execution pathway nonexistent.

novelty.rationale: The core intervention is a mature, globally standardized 'Walk/Bike to Work' campaign. The proposed 'innovation' of layering a custom app for data tracking adds no novel technical or governance value, as similar data aggregation is standard in existing platforms (Strava, Citymapper). The proposal repackages an existing awareness campaign without introducing a new causal mechanism for behavioral change.

impact.rationale: The impact projections are scientifically and methodologically flawed. The proposal conflates a one-day transient event with long-term structural policy outcomes, falsely attributing massive CO2 savings from solar laws and bus fleets to a single walking day. Furthermore, the directive to 'skip the bus' actively undermines resilience by discouraging low-carbon public transit in favor of walking, which is inaccessible to the majority, creating a regressive impact that excludes vulnerable populations.

presentation.rationale: While the proposal uses standard structural elements, the internal logic is fractured, oscillating between a 'practice' and a 'project' without a clear governance bridge. The reliance on broken image placeholders, external links, and optimistic language that masks deep structural flaws (e.g., ignoring urban sprawl) weakens the document's credibility. The tone assumes 'altruism' will cover hard costs without addressing the high transaction costs of public-private coordination.

final.synthesis: Both clusters (S1 and S2) reached a near-unanimous consensus on the critical failures of this proposal, resulting in a final judgment of 1.5. The primary disagreement was not on the *existence* of flaws, but on the *severity* of the impact and the potential for salvage. S1 focused on the logical unsoundness of the causal chain (event -> legislation) and the lack of budget. S2 added a critical socio-spatial dimension, arguing that the 'skip the bus' directive is not just impractical but actively harmful (regressive) to low-income populations in the periphery. I resolved this by accepting S2's argument: the impact is not merely low (1) but potentially negative due to the exclusionary nature of the advice. Regarding the 'Big Data' component, while S2 noted a minor tension about its potential value if the 'skip the bus' rule were removed, I decided against any salvage attempt because the proposal's foundational premise (a single day triggering systemic change) is a causal fallacy that cannot be fixed by removing one directive. The 'Google app' dependency and the lack of a funding model remain absolute barriers. Thus, the scores are locked at the lowest feasible levels for Feasibility and Impact, with Novelty and Presentation reflecting the repackaging of standard ideas with insufficient rigor.
final.uncertainty: The only remaining uncertainty lies in the theoretical potential of the 'Big Data' component if the proposal were radically re-scoped to remove the 'skip the bus' directive and the false causal links to legislation. However, as currently written, the proposal is a 'single point of failure' dependent on a non-existent Google partnership and government mandates. The disagreement between S1 and S2 regarding the 'symbolic' value of the event was resolved by rejecting the notion that symbolism outweighs the active harm of discouraging public transit. No ambiguity remains regarding the mathematical invalidity of the global extrapolation or the operational impossibility of the execution plan.
